coredata.dc:title: A soluble-fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer sensor based on combined soluble solid phase-and liquid–liquid-microextraction
coredata.dc:creator: Manshaei, Faranak
Bagheri, Habib
coredata.dc:description: The rigid and highly cross-linked nature of fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer (FMIP) make it dispersive in solution and cause scattering of source light which limits the application of FMIP in photoluminescence sensors. In addition, the distribution of FMIPs in the solution is unstable and in some cases their rapid settlement deviate the accuracy and precision of measurement. In this work, a miniaturized probe based on the soluble-fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer (S-FSMIP) was developed and conveniently implemented to detect sertraline (SER), as a model target, in human urine. The developed probe synthesis was conducted in two main steps: i) design of FSMIP with hydrophobic core and hydrophilic surface, ii) swelling process of FSMIP at a high temperature in the presence of a high boiling hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent (HDES). Employment of HDES as a green solvent for solvation of FSMIP minimizes the consumption of hazardous chemicals and also improves analyte transfer to surface of FMIP. The adsorption isotherm and adsorption kinetic of the S-FSMIP were also studied and compared with FSMIP. Accordingly, the analytical parameters such as linearity, limits of detection, limits of quantification, relative standard deviations and the absolute recovery values were provided for this rapid and green-oriented method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Soluble-fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer sensor
Miniaturized fluorescence assay
Hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent
Carbon dots
Sertraline
Sensing mechanism
Adsorption isotherm and kinetic

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Soluble solid phase combined with liquid–liquid microextraction for selective detection of SER in human urine
Characterization of HDES, CDs, FSMIP, S-FSMIP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduates School of Sharif University of Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Research Council (Grant number QA990417) and Graduates School of Sharif University of Technology (SUT) is acknowledged for supporting this project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram for the extraction setup implemented for the determination of SER.

(A) The FT-IR spectra of thymol (yellow), menthol (red) and HDES (orange), (B) The FT-IR spectrum of the green-emitting CDs, (C) The FT-IR spectra of poly (MAA-co-DVB) (green), fluorescent poly(MAA-co-DVB) microspheres (blue) and FSMIP (purple), (D) The FT-IR spectra of HDES (orange), FSMIP (purple) and S-FSMIP (pink).

(A) The size distribution analysis of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere, (B) FSMIP and (C) S-FSMIP, (D) TGA and DTA spectra of HDES, and (E) The XRD patterns of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere (orange) and FSMIP (blue).

(A) The absorption spectra of green-emitting CDs (blue), S-FSMIP (orange), fluorescence excitation (red), and emission (green) spectra of S-FSMIP; (B) The fluorescence emission of S-FSMIP at different λex; (C) The fluorescence green emission of FSMIP (brown powder) under a UV lamp compared with poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere (white powder) and (D) Comparison of analytical response (F0/F) of SER with various drugs (200 times concentrated) and investigation of selectivity.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra, (B) Stern-Volmer plot of the developed sensor in the presence of different concentrations of SER, and (C) image of the fluorescence quenching of the developed sensor at a concentration of 400 ng mL−1 under a UV lamp.
The obtained Langmuir and Freundlich parameters.
Relative recoveries of SER obtained from different real samples.

head.ce:title: A soluble-fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer sensor based on combined soluble solid phase-and liquid–liquid-microextraction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11365-9516, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, P.O. Box 11365-9516, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rigid and highly cross-linked nature of fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer (FMIP) make it dispersive in solution and cause scattering of source light which limits the application of FMIP in photoluminescence sensors. In addition, the distribution of FMIPs in the solution is unstable and in some cases their rapid settlement deviate the accuracy and precision of measurement. In this work, a miniaturized probe based on the soluble-fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer (S-FSMIP) was developed and conveniently implemented to detect sertraline (SER), as a model target, in human urine. The developed probe synthesis was conducted in two main steps: i) design of FSMIP with hydrophobic core and hydrophilic surface, ii) swelling process of FSMIP at a high temperature in the presence of a high boiling hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent (HDES). Employment of HDES as a green solvent for solvation of FSMIP minimizes the consumption of hazardous chemicals and also improves analyte transfer to surface of FMIP. The adsorption isotherm and adsorption kinetic of the S-FSMIP were also studied and compared with FSMIP. Accordingly, the analytical parameters such as linearity, limits of detection, limits of quantification, relative standard deviations and the absolute recovery values were provided for this rapid and green-oriented method.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Soluble-fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer sensor

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data are provided in the manuscript and supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:para: ‘COVID-19-related depression’ is a mental health crisis which has emerged with the outbreak of Coronavirus disease [1]. Recently, different theories have been suggested that some antidepressants not only improve depressive symptoms but also reduce the risk of death and intubation due to Covid-19 [2]. Therefore, the development of methods to identify and track antidepressants in patients is one of the necessities to help the treating physician to continue the treatment protocols. SER is a member of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) that is deeply recommended for patients as a potential therapeutic option for COVID-19 [3].

While researchers are striving to develop new methodologies of tracking different drugs in clinical laboratories, it is important to overcome the practical limitations of previous methods. Fluorescence-based methods are among these developed methodologies which have received much attention today due to their sensitivity, simplicity and cost-efficiency. In these methods, the analyte identification performs through the interaction of the target analyte with the used fluorophore which effects on the fluorescence behavior of the fluorophore [4,5]. High photostability, narrow and tunable emission spectrum, high quantum efficiency, and wide absorption spectrum are favorable properties of the used fluorophore in these techniques. Among the available fluorophores, carbon dots (CDs) with their unique properties are the most suitable choice. CDs also have good biocompatibility, robust chemical inertness and low toxicity which is approved by Green Chemistry [6,7]. One of the challenging issues in the fluorescence-based methods is the induction of selectivity for specific interaction of fluorophore with the desired analyte. Various strategies have been reported to induce selectivity, most notably the use of antibodies or aptamers as a specific agent for analyte detection. Despite the specific function of antibodies, they suffer from being labor intensive wash-based assay, time-consuming procedures, potential for high background, requirement of expensive reagents and instruments [8], being sensitive to temperature changes, short signal stability, the complexity of functionalizing them with fluorophore species without altering or reducing the specific function of antibody and multi-step sample preparation are some issues of optical sensors based on antibody [9].

Recently, fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer (FMIP) as combination of molecularly imprinted techniques (MIT) with fluorescence-based sensor has been introduced for use in high selective and sensitive recognition. Incomplete template removal, small binding capacity, poor site accessibility and low affinity are limitations of conventional molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) [5]. Surface molecularly imprinted polymer (SMIP) as innovative technique has been developed to overcome this limitation [5,10]. In this technique, various particles including silica particles [11], Fe3O4 nanoparticles [12], polymer beads [13] and other materials [14,15] were used as support material of SMIP and imprinting process of analyte was performed on the surface of support substrate.

Fluorescent surface molecularly imprinted polymer (FSMIP) could be implemented as both extractive phase and also photoluminescence sensor with no need to expensive devices such as chromatographic device [5]. The application of FMIP in photoluminescence sensors might be limited because the rigid and highly cross-linked nature of FMIP, make it dispersive in solution and cause scattering of source light. In addition, the distribution of FMIPs in the solution is unstable and in some cases they settle quickly which effect on accuracy and precision of measurement. According to the research of Liang et al. [16], by swelling the traditional MIP at a high temperature in the presence of high boiling solvent, soluble-MIP (S-MIP) could be obtained. Inspired by this investigation, we report here for the first time a stable soluble-FSMIP (S-FSMIP)-based sensor.

Based on our previous study [17], deep eutectic solvents (DES), known as green media, has potential to be implemented as solvent and doping agent to improve the efficiency of fluorescence sensor. In addition, there are some publications which confirm the applicability of DES as extractive solvent. The hydrophilicity of DES limits its realistic utility to only polar compounds, which is a first-rate drawback. Hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents (HDES) have emerged as an opportunity for extractive media able to extract non-polar [18,19], moderately polar organic [20] and inorganic molecules from aqueous environments [21]. The synthesis of HDES is similar to hydrophilic DES and forms via the hydrogen bond formation at a certain molar eutectic ratio, while hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) or hydrogen bond donor (HBD) and/or both have hydrophobic nature [19,22,23].

Herein, we firstly synthesized poly (methacrylic acid-co-divinylbenzene) microspheres and subsequently functionalized with green emission CDs and used as support substrate of FSMIP. In second step, they were coated by silicon as surface imprinting substrate. Finally, the synthesized FSMIP was swelled in the presence of thymol: menthol HDES at high temperature and S-FSMIP was obtained. Thymol contributes as HBD and menthol as HBA at molar ratio of 1:1 [24]. The obtained S-FSMIP was implemented as fluorescent probe to detect SER in human urine. The novelty of this method is the synthesis of S-FSMIP in the presence of HDES which overcome the limitation of FSMIP. Implementation of HDES as green solvent for swelling of FSMIP and assistant agent to the extractive phase led to the consumption of least amount of chemicals. The presence of HDES improves the transfer of analyte from sample matrix to imprinted cavity of S-FSMIP. A selective fluorescence quenching pathway is originated due to the hydrogen bond formation between SER and CDs and subsequently SER could be tracked as fluorescence emission of CDs is turned off. Based on the reported results, this strategy is a selective, rapid and feasible approach to design a biocompatible, sensitive, time- and cost-effective fluorescence sensor for the detection of SER. The investigation of the adsorption kinetics confirms the chemisorption of analyte on S-FSMIP.

Thymol (99 wt% purity) and menthol (99.5 wt% purity) were purchased from Acros Organics. Divinylbenzene (80 %, DVB), 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (98 %, AIBN), ethylenediamine (for synthesis, EDA), sodium chloride (extra pure, NaCl), sodium hydroxide (98 %, NaOH), methacrylic acid (99 %, MAA), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (98 %, EDC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (98 %, NHS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol, citric acid anhydrous (CA), hydrochloric acid (37 %, HCl), potassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (98 %, APTES) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (for synthesis, TEOS) were provided from Merck. Sertraline (SER), propranolol (PRO), citalopram (CIT) were gifted from Tolid Daru pharmaceutical company. Haloperidol (HAL) was purchased from Marham Daru pharmaceutical company.

Fresh spinach was purchased from local supermarket to synthesize CDs. The urine samples from a healthy person and four patients (3 females and 1 male) were collected in polyethylene containers and kept at 4 °C. The stock solution of SER and HAL was prepared in methanol at 1000 μg mL−1 and stored in the refrigerator. The stock solution of PRO and CIT was prepared in ethanol at 1000 μg mL−1 and stored in the refrigerator Working standard solutions were daily prepared by diluting the stock solution.

The poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere was synthesized by precipitation polymerization according to the literature with some modification [25]. Briefly, 2 g MAA, 3 g DVB and 23 mg AIBN as free-radical initiator were dissolved in 100 mL acetonitrile/toluene (volume ratio: 75/25) and sonicated for 5 min to form a clear solution. After homogenization, the solution was purged with N2 gas and sealed under the N2 atmosphere. Finally, the sealed flask was placed into the oil bath, heated at 70 °C and kept under constant magnetic stirring for 24 h (Fig. S1A). After 24 h, the obtained microspheres were separated by centrifugation at a speed of 5000 rpm for 10 min. Then, particles were washed with 100 mL acetic acid/methanol solution (15/85 by volume), and subsequently 100 mL pure methanol and finally dried in oven at 60 °C for 24 h.

The synthesis process of green-emitting CDs is mentioned in detail in the Supporting Information. 100 mg poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere was dispersed into 14 mL phosphate buffer (0.01 M, PH 7.4) and 223 mg EDC was added and stirred for 30 min to active carboxylic acid group of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere. After 30 min, 1 mL phosphate buffer containing 134 mg NHS was added to solution and stirred for 90 min. Finally, 10 mL synthesized green-emitting CDs solution was added and kept under constant magnetic stirring for overnight at room temperature and darkness. To obtain the fluorescent poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere, the resulting mixture was centrifuged, washed with methanol to remove excess reagents and dried at room temperature and darkness.

As mentioned earlier, the hydrogen bonds formed between HBA and HBD compounds at a certain molar ratio synthesize HDES. Menthol is the sustainable, inexpensive and hydrophobic compound which could be easily extracted from Mentha species. Thymol is a natural monoterpene obtained from thyme oil or other volatile oils and has antiseptic, antibacterial, and antifungal actions. Herein, menthol as HBA and thymol as HBD were chosen and mixed at eutectic ratio of 1:1 and heated to achieve a clear liquid at 60 °C (Fig. S2).
The synthesis procedure of S-FSMIP involves two steps: 1) synthesis of FSMIP (Fig. S1B) and 2) swelling of FSMIP at high temperature in the presence of HDES (Fig. S1C).

In the first step, 30 mg fluorescent poly(MAA-co-DVB) microsphere were dispersed in 10 mL DI water and then added 50 mg SER to react with carboxylic group of CDs embedded on poly(MAA-co-DVB) microsphere and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 30 μL APTES was added to the reaction mixture and stirred for 1 h. Then, 20 μL TEOS and 30 μL NH3 were added at 25 °C and the reaction was stirred for 16 h. The obtained FSMIP were separated by centrifugation and then washed several times with DI water and methanol to remove the excess monomers and analyte (template). The fluorescence property of FSMIP in the presence of template was quenched. After each washing step, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced through removing template from the structure. Therefore, the washing process was continued until the fluorescence intensity remained constant under the UV-lamp. Then, the obtained FSMIP was dried at 60 °C for 12 h under the dark condition. To comparison, fluorescent surface non-imprinted polymer (FSNIP) was synthesized by the similar procedure in the absence of template.

In the second step, 30 mg from the prepared FSMIP was added to 1 mL thymol: menthol HDES and then the mixture was heated at 120 °C under constant magnetic stirring for 8 h. The concentration of obtained S-FSMIP is 30000 mg L−1. The images of obtained S-FSMIP and dispersive FSMIP in water are shown in Fig. S3. For preparation of soluble-fluorescent surface non-imprinted polymer (S-FSNIP), the synthesized FSNIP was used by the similar procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soluble solid phase combined with liquid–liquid microextraction for selective detection of SER in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL diluted urine sample (1:2, urine:DI water) of healthy person was transferred to 2-mL Eppendorf microtube. Then, SER was added to Eppendorf microtube in order to adjust the concentration of SER to remain at 350 ng mL−1 in the diluted urine sample. The pH of urine sample was adjusted to 10 with NaOH (0.1 M) and included 10 % (w/v) NaCl. Then, 300 μL optimized S-FSMIP with concentration of 3000 mg L−1 was added to urine sample and stirred to form vortex for 10 min. After 10 min, S-FSMIP was separated from urine sample by microfuge. Finally, S-FSMIP phase was carefully transferred to the microcell of spectrofluorometric device and the signal was recorded and expressed by F. The fluorescence intensity of S-FSMIP phase before extraction process was recorded and expressed by F0. The analytical signal (F0/F) is attributed to ratio of the fluorescence intensity of S-FSMIP before the extraction process (F0) to the fluorescence intensity (F) after the extraction of SER. The schematic of extraction process is shown in Fig. 1 .

In the present study, FSMIP with hydrophobic core and hydrophilic imprinted surface was designed and subsequently was swelled in HDES which make formation of S-FSMIP. The prepared S-FSMIP is a suggested practical approach to overcome application restrictions of fluorescence signal recording in dispersive fluorescent solid methods. In addition, the implementation of HDES significantly improved the analyte transfer from sample matrix to FSMIP and rapidity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of HDES, CDs, FSMIP, S-FSMIP
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectrum of thymol (yellow, Fig. 2 A) includes phenol-OH peaks centered at 3225 cm−1, CH stretching vibrations at 2959 and 2868 cm−1, aromatic CC stretching at 1622 and 1521 cm−1, CH out of-plane bending at 805 cm−1, CO stretching at 1243, and OH bending vibrations at 1351 cm−1. In addition, the presence of a tri-substituted aromatic ring is confirmed by the overtones of the aromatic CH bending at 2230–1942 cm−1 [26]. The spectrum of menthol (red, Fig. 2A) shows OH stretching vibrations at 3260 cm−1, CH stretching at 2959 and 2873 cm−1, CO stretching at 1226 and 995 cm−1, CC stretching mode of the hexagonal ring at 846–975 cm−1 and CH bending vibrations at 1368 to 1453 cm−1 [27]. The hydrogen bonds between menthol and thymol are the main reason for the HDES formation. Therefore, the intermolecular hydrogen bonding between thymol molecules decreases in the presence of menthol, and the OH stretching vibration shifts to higher wavenumbers for HDES. As shown in Fig. 2A (orange), the OH stretching vibration in menthol, thymol, and HDES occurs at 3260, 3225, and 3342 cm−1, respectively. This is a strong evidence to confirm a new hydrogen bond and subsequently new compound formation [17,28].

The FT-IR spectrum of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere was also recorded (green, Fig. 2C). The observed peak at 3084 cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic strain bands of the CH bond of the vinyl group and the bonds corresponding to flexion are observable around 906 and 990 cm−1. The peaks at 710, 799 and 836 cm−1 are attributed to the aromatic CH bonding and the axial deformation vibration of CC is observed at 1603 cm−1. The bands appeared at 1709 and 3442 cm−1 are the characteristic peaks of asymmetric vibration of the CO group and deformation vibration band of the OH group which are related to the MAA. The observed peaks at the range of 1216–1218 cm−1 and 2923–2937 cm−1 are also related to the symmetrical vibration of the CO group and the asymmetric symmetrical CH stretching bond, respectively [25].

The FT-IR spectrum of the green-emitting CDs is illustrated in Fig. 2B. The peaks at 3500 and 1556 cm−1 are attributed to the NH stretching and NH bending, respectively. These characteristic bands confirm the presence of amine groups on the surface of CDs. To provide the convincing evidence on the successful synthesis of the fluorescent poly(MAA-co-DVB) microsphere, its FT-IR spectra was studied. As shown in Fig. 2C (blue), the peak at 1680 cm−1 is assigned as stretching vibration of CO in secondary amide compounds while the NH bending vibration band is also appeared at 1560 cm−1. The obtained data confirm the functionalization of poly(MAA-co-DVB) microsphere by CDs and subsequent effective synthesis of the fluorescent poly(MAA-co-DVB) microspheres.

The composition of the synthesized FSMIP was also characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy to observe the stretching vibration of the Si-O-Si group in the region 1100–1000 cm−1 [5,12]. As shown in Fig. 2C (purple), the obvious absorption peak at approximately 1034 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of Si-O-Si which confirms the formation of silica coating on the fluorescent poly(MAA-co-DVB) microspheres. After swelling FSMIP in the presence of HDES, FT-IR spectrum of the obtained S-FSMIP was also recorded. As shown in Fig. 2D (pink), the FT-IR spectrum of S-FSMIP is similar to HDES and the presence of bands at 1680 and 1709 cm−1 in the spectrum are evidence of the FSMIP presence.

The particle size distribution of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere, FSMIP and S-FSMIP was investigated by dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique. As it seen in Fig. 3 A, B and C, the size distribution of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microspheres, FSMIP and S-FSMIP are 0.025–0.243, 0.051–0.578 and 0.409–6.540 μm, respectively. The increased FSMIP particle size compared with poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere could confirm the formation of hydrophilic surface on the poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere. Besides, the difference in particle size of S-FSMIP with FSMIP is probably due to the swelling of FSMIP in the presence of HDES.

Swelling of FSMIP in the presence of HDES at high temperature is the main step to synthesize S-FSMIP. The thermal stability of FSMIP was therefore investigated by TGA. In Fig. 3D, the TGA and DTA thermograms obtained from FSMIP are shown. In TGA analysis, the sample was continually weighted while heating (until 500 °C with a rate of 20 °C min−1), as an inert gas atmosphere was passed over it. As shown in Fig. 3D, FSMIP resists high temperature (≈300 °C) and its decomposition starts after this temperature. Therefore, the heat resistance of FSMIP is appropriate for the synthesis of S-FSMIP, since the usual temperature for swelling is lower than 300 °C.

A broad intense peak at 16.84° together with a low intense peak at 41.08° were obtained in the XRD pattern of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere (orange, Fig. 3E) which indicate its amorphous nature and the grafting of MAA and DVB, respectively. These diffraction peaks are also apparent in the obtained XRD pattern of FSMIP (blue, Fig. 3E), which confirm the presence of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere in the FSMIP structure. The lower intensity and shift to the higher angles of these diffraction peaks are due to the coating of microsphere with SiO2 in FSMIP [29]. Additionally, the presence of the broad intense peak at around 2θ = 28 in the XRD pattern of FSMIP is originated from the SiO2 coating.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of green-emitting CDs (blue, Fig. 4 A) and S-FSMIP (orange, Fig. 4A) were recorded in the 200–1100 nm interval. The intensive peak appeared at 350 nm is possibly due to the n-π* transition of the CO bond of CDs. This peak shows a red shift from 350 to 425 nm when the green-emitting CDs are coated with silica surface in FSMIP. By investigating the photoluminescence of S-FSMIP (Fig. 4A), the peak at 490 nm with green fluorescence emitting at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm is observed. The fluorescence spectra of S-FSMIP (2000 mg L−1) at various λex are illustrated in Fig. 4B. The excitation-independent emission of S-FSMIP somehow confirms that the activated groups of CDs are rather uniform and distributed narrowly [17]. The observed red-shift was noticeable by increasing the λex from 420 to 450 nm. The fluorescence green emission of the synthesized FSMIP (brown powder) shown under a UV lamp and compared with emission of poly (MAA-co-DVB) microsphere (Fig. 4C).

The data relevant to the optimization process are shown in the Supporting Information (Fig. S4 and Table S1). The optimization condition consisted of 1:2 urine diluting factor, 1:1.5 functional monomer: cross linker ratio, 3000 mg L−1 concentration of S-FSMIP, 10 % (v/w) NaCl salt, 10 sample pH and 10 min extraction time.

Haloperidol (HAL), citalopram (CIT) and propranolol (PRO) are drugs which might be prescribed along with SER. Therefore, their interference on the analytical performance of the method was evaluated. For each selectivity experiment, the final concentration of SER and three other drugs was 80 ng mL−1 and 16 μg mL−1 (200 folds), respectively. As shown in Fig. 4D, high selectivity was obtained for SER on the synthesized S-FSMIP, indicating that the compound selectivity was in the following order: SER > PRO > CIT > HAL. The presence of NH group in PRO might be the reason of more selectivity of S-FSMIP compared with CIT and HAL. In S-FSNIP, the higher signals were obtained for interference drugs which relate to their 200 folds concentration.

There are two types of interactions which S-FSMIP could extract SER from urine sample. They involves: 1) specific interactions via cavities imprinted on FSMIP, and 2) non-specific interactions via hydrogen bond formation and hydrophobic interactions. In the present work, SER was detected by fluorescence quenching of S-FSMIP. The adsorption of SER on the cavities imprinted on S-FSMIP might enhance the hydrogen bond formation between oxygen atoms of CDs in S-FSMIP and the amine group (NH) of sertraline. The hydrogen bond formation leads to the electron transfer from S-FSMIP to SER and subsequent fluorescence quenching. CDs contain extensive quantity of amino and hydroxyl groups and the resultant negatively charged surface, pH 10, which affect the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions with the –NH group of SER and the consequent emission quenching. Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) is probably the primary quenching route that leads to electron transfer from the electron-rich moieties to the electron-deficient analyte [30,31]. By treating CDs as a semiconductor, the excited electrons, under UV irradiation, would return to the valence band (VB) from conduction band (CB), accompanying with the fluorescence illumination. When SER is captured into the S-FSMIP system through imprinted cavities with specific recognition sites, upon its contacts with CDs, they are bonded together via the hydrogen bonding process. Considering the electron-withdrawing nature of the N atom in SER molecule, the excited electrons in CB are transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of SER, leading to the fluorescence quenching of S-FSMIP. In this process, the SER molecule and S-FSMIP act as electron receptor and donor, respectively. Briefly, the sensing mechanism based on the fluorescence quenching in S-FSMIP system might be due to the charge transfer phenomenon [29]. S-FSMIP was synthesized by swelling of FSMIP in HDES at high temperature. There are two types of interactions to form S-FSMIP: 1) hydrogen bond formation between FSMIP and HDES and 2) hydrophobic and π-π interactions between core of FSMIP and HDES. The obtained data in FT-IR spectrum of S-FSMIP shows there is no hydrogen bond formation between FSMIP and HDES and second type of interaction is possibly the main reason for the S-FSMIP formation. The possibility of fluorescence quenching due to the hydrogen bond formation between FSMIP and HDES is therefore negligible while, the inherent viscosity of DES improves the fluorescence intensity by reducing the internal conversion process.

The adsorption isotherms of Langmuir and Freundlich were studied at equilibrium time to obtain adsorption mechanism, capacity and affinity of the developed S-FSMIP and compare it with FSMIP (Table 1 ). The R2 values for SER, originated from the Langmuir and Freundlich models are approximately 0.99 and 0.87 over the S-FSMIP, respectively. Considering these values it could be concluded that the adsorption of SER on S-FSMIP is fitted quite well to the Langmuir model of monolayer adsorption. In Freundlich model, n represents the degree of the surface heterogeneity and distribution of the adsorbed molecules. The higher intensity of the adsorption will obtain in greater value of n (n > 1) [32]. The higher value of n for S-FSMIP compared with FSMIP is an authentic proof indicating the superiority of S-FSMIP by increasing the intensity of the adsorption. The adsorption isotherm studies are expressed in detail in the Supporting Information. Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption curves of S-FSMIP, FSMIP and S-FSNIP for SER were shown in Fig. S5.

To confirm the adsorption mechanism of S-FSMIP and compare it with FSMIP, two adsorption kinetic models were investigated and expressed in detail in the Supporting Information. Adsorption kinetic plots of S-FSMIP and FSMIP were shown in Fig. S6. Considering the data listed in Table S2 and lower value of the determination coefficient (R2) of the first order kinetic, the sorption mechanism follows the second order model revealing that the chemisorption mechanism is more likely to be dominant, confirming the hydrogen bond formation between S-FSMIP and SER. The obtained data indicates that the rate of adsorption was enhanced with utilization of S-FSMIP compared with FSMIP.

The Stern-Volmer equation describes the fluorescence quenching (Eq S1). For this work, the Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) was found to be 0.0604 mL ng−1. To examine the whole method applicability, limits of detection (LOD, S/N = 3), limits of quantification (LOQ, S/N = 10), linear dynamic range (LDR), coefficient of determination (R2), intra-day and inter-day precision (relative standard deviations, RSD%) are listed in Table S3. The fluorescence spectra and the Stern-Volmer plots at the presence of various amounts of SER are shown in Fig. 5 A. By calculating the F-test and considering the RSD% of the lower and upper concentration ranges, the Fexperiment was less than Fcritical by depicting the unweighted calibration plot (Fig. 5B). The image of fluorescence quenching at the SER concentration of 400 ng mL−1 under a UV lamp is shown in Fig. 5C.

To investigate the applicability of the current method, four urine samples from patients who were under treatment with SER were analyzed and the presence of this drug was detected. The urine sample was collected 8 h after the last SER tablet consumption and diluted twofold with DI water as the only pretreatment before analysis. Control urine samples (without SER) were analyzed by spectrofluoremetry under the optimum condition after being fortified with SER at 2, 25 and 50 ng mL−1 levels. The absolute recovery (AR%) results are shown in Table 2 . Our results are well comparable with other relevant methods used for detection of SER (Table S4).

The design of the S-FSMIP based on swelling of FSMIP in the presence of HDES is the innovative strategy of the present work. By developing this approach, the issues of sensors based on the dispersive FMIP including scattering and internal conversion between the fluorophore molecules are reduced and subsequently the sensitivity is improved. The presence of HDES in the suggested sensor is an effective approach to enhance the extraction efficiency which is confirmed by increasing the adsorption capacity of S-FSMIP compared with FSMIP. In addition, the design of the developed sensor in a miniaturized scale (300 μL) with a small amount of urine sample (1 mL) and implementation of DES are encouraging approaches toward green chemistry. The satisfaction on the characterization and analytical data are also confirmative evidences for the applicability of the S-FSMIP sensor to detect SER via fluorescence quenching. Based on the adsorption studies, the relatively low adsorption capacity of the developed S-FSMIP is an identified limitation which could enhance through the induction of the porosity on the FSMIP such as development of the mesoporous structures. Type of HDES with different compositions is another predicted strategy to improve the adsorption capacity and subsequently extraction efficiency which could be investigated in future works.
Faranak Manshaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Habib Bagheri: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Research Council (Grant number QA990417) and Graduates School of Sharif University of Technology (SUT) is acknowledged for supporting this project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108194.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
